Rating: Game Delivery Velocity
1 - Lagging: Delays are frequent; game milestones consistently slip by weeks. Frame rate targets unmet. WebGL builds fail regularly. Backend/Creative collaboration reactive and ineffective.
2 - Developing: Milestones occasionally slip; some frame rate instability. WebGL builds work but require rework. Collaboration exists but often ad-hoc and reactive.
3 - Proficient: Game milestones achieved on schedule. Frame rates stable at target (60 fps). WebGL builds reliable and optimized. Effective collaboration with Backend for multiplayer systems and Creative for asset integration.
4 - Exemplary: Consistently delivers early or on-time with buffer. Frame rates exceed targets across devices. WebGL builds are fully automated and performant. Cross-team collaboration proactive; multiplayer/leaderboard integration seamless.

Rating: Predictability & Scope Management
1 - Lagging: Game scope constantly expands uncontrolled. Estimates unreliable by >50%. No clear scope boundaries; feature creep unpredictable. Stakeholders surprised repeatedly.
2 - Developing: Scope expands moderately; estimates off by 20-30%. Some scope boundaries defined but frequently negotiated. Occasional stakeholder surprises.
3 - Proficient: Game scope clearly defined upfront. Estimates accurate within 15%. Scope creep identified and managed proactively. Stakeholders regularly informed of scope changes.
4 - Exemplary: Game scope locked with buffer. Estimates within 10%. Scope changes rare; when they occur, tradeoffs transparently negotiated with full stakeholder buy-in.

Rating: Quality & Stability (Frame Rates, WebGL, Browser Compatibility)
1 - Lagging: Frame rates erratic (20-45 fps). WebGL performance poor on mobile. Browser compatibility issues (Chrome/Firefox/Safari gaps). Game crashes frequent.
2 - Developing: Frame rates inconsistent (45-55 fps). WebGL works on major browsers but with compromises. Minor browser-specific bugs. Few user-impacting crashes.
3 - Proficient: Frame rates stable (58-60 fps). WebGL optimized for modern browsers. Browser compatibility tested and documented. Crashes rare and quickly resolved.
4 - Exemplary: Frame rates solid (60 fps+) across devices/browsers. WebGL highly optimized (minimal draw calls, texture atlasing). Compatibility span includes legacy browsers. Proactive performance monitoring.

Rating: Cross-Team Collaboration (Backend, Creative)
1 - Lagging: Collaboration minimal; silos prevalent. Backend multiplayer/leaderboard integration delayed. Creative assets don't match game vision. Handoffs chaotic.
2 - Developing: Collaboration present but reactive. Backend integration works but inefficient. Creative assets mostly align; some rework needed. Handoffs functional but with friction.
3 - Proficient: Regular collaboration cadence. Backend integration smooth; multiplayer/leaderboards working. Creative assets align with design intent. Handoffs defined and efficient.
4 - Exemplary: Proactive daily standups with Backend/Creative. Backend systems scale seamlessly. Asset pipelines optimized end-to-end. Handoffs frictionless; cross-team ownership high.

Rating: Team Allocation (Programmers vs Designer vs 3D Modelers)
1 - Lagging: Team roles unclear; 3D modelers idle while programmers bottlenecked. Designer overloaded with multiple games. No clear ownership of subsystems. 7-person team underutilized.
2 - Developing: Team roles defined but not optimized. Occasional role misalignment (e.g., modelers waiting for programmer bandwidth). Designer stretched across 2 games. Some subsystem ownership gaps.
3 - Proficient: Team roles well-defined: ~4 programmers, 1 designer, 2 modelers. Each subsystem has clear owner. Capacity balanced; roles leverage skills. Designer focused on primary game.
4 - Exemplary: Team roles optimized dynamically. Programmers specialize (networking, graphics, gameplay). Designer and modelers cross-train. Surge capacity available for crunch. Role flexibility high.

Rating: Capacity Management (7-Person Team)
1 - Lagging: Team consistently overbooked; burnout visible. No capacity buffer. Absences/vacations cause project collapse. No workload planning.
2 - Developing: Team near capacity most sprints. Limited buffer. Absences create strain; backups unavailable. Ad-hoc workload planning.
3 - Proficient: Team capacity tracked in ClickUp. 15-20% buffer maintained. Absences covered by cross-trained peers. Quarterly capacity planning.
4 - Exemplary: Capacity modeled per role. 25-30% strategic buffer. Absences result in zero disruption. Workload balanced; team energy high. Proactive staffing for crunch.

Rating: Tooling & Infrastructure (Unity Licenses, Build Pipelines, Asset Pipelines)
1 - Lagging: Unity licenses inadequate or expired. Build times 15+ minutes. Asset pipeline manual and error-prone. CI/CD nonexistent. Build failures block releases.
2 - Developing: Some Unity licenses; build times 8-10 minutes. Asset pipeline partially automated. CI/CD exists but unreliable. Occasional build failures.
3 - Proficient: Sufficient Unity licenses (Pro for lead devs). Build times 3-5 minutes. Asset pipeline automated. CI/CD reliable; WebGL exports consistent. Occasional minor hiccups.
4 - Exemplary: Licenses optimized and justified. Build times <2 minutes (incremental). Asset pipeline fully automated (texture atlasing, LOD generation, compression). CI/CD bulletproof; one-click deploys.

Rating: Cost Efficiency (Asset Costs, Tooling Spend)
1 - Lagging: Asset purchases uncontrolled; redundant licenses. Tooling spend unjustified. Budget blown regularly. No spend visibility.
2 - Developing: Asset spend tracked loosely. Some tool redundancy. Budget occasionally exceeded. Limited justification for purchases.
3 - Proficient: Asset spend reviewed quarterly. Tooling consolidated and justified. Budget predictable. ROI documented for major purchases.
4 - Exemplary: Asset spend optimized (reusable libraries, open-source assets). Tooling cost-justified with clear ROI. Budget underrun or zero variance. Negotiated discounts (Unity for small teams).

Rating: Communication & Transparency (Milestone Demos)
1 - Lagging: No regular demos. Stakeholders surprised by game state. Design decisions unexplained. Progress visibility zero. Team alignment weak.
2 - Developing: Demos quarterly or less. Stakeholders see game but with surprises. Design rationale sometimes explained. Progress tracked loosely. Team alignment partial.
3 - Proficient: Bi-weekly milestone demos showing playable builds. Stakeholders understand game state. Design decisions documented and communicated. Progress visible in ClickUp. Team aligned weekly.
4 - Exemplary: Weekly playable demos showcasing new mechanics. Stakeholders engaged and informed. Design rationale transparent. Real-time progress visibility. Team alignment daily.

Rating: Expectation Management (Scope Creep Prevention)
1 - Lagging: Scope creep uncontrolled; stakeholders add features mid-sprint. No 'Definition of Done'. Game vision constantly shifts. Team frustrated by goal-post movement.
2 - Developing: Scope creep common; stakeholders negotiate features. Loose 'Definition of Done'. Game vision somewhat drifting. Team occasionally frustrated.
3 - Proficient: Scope locked per milestone. Features triaged; low-priority deferred. Clear 'Definition of Done'. Game vision stable. Stakeholders respect boundaries.
4 - Exemplary: Scope ironclad; all feature requests go through triage. 'Definition of Done' enforced rigorously. Game vision unwavering; vision document regularly revisited. Stakeholders trust roadmap completely.

Rating: Alignment with Product Vision (Game Design Vision)
1 - Lagging: No clear game design vision or vision document. Team builds features without direction. Game identity confused. Vision constantly re-interpreted.
2 - Developing: Loose game design vision; document outdated or informal. Team generally aligned but re-interpretation occurs. Game identity emerging but inconsistent.
3 - Proficient: Game Design Document (GDD) clear and current. Vision: 'casual web-based puzzle game' (or similar). Team deeply aligned on mechanics/art style/target player. Game identity strong.
4 - Exemplary: GDD comprehensive and living. Vision compelling and inspirational. Team embodies game vision in every decision. Vision reviewed quarterly and evolved strategically.

Rating: Feedback Integration (Playtesting Feedback)
1 - Lagging: Playtesting minimal or ad-hoc. Feedback ignored or poorly documented. Players frustrated; engagement low. No iteration loop.
2 - Developing: Playtesting monthly. Feedback captured but prioritization weak. Some iteration. Player satisfaction improving.
3 - Proficient: Playtesting bi-weekly with 10-15 external players. Feedback documented in ClickUp; triaged for iteration. Player engagement metrics tracked. Rapid iteration cycle.
4 - Exemplary: Playtesting weekly; robust feedback loops. Player personas defined; feedback segmented by player type. Engagement metrics drive decisions. Quick iteration; A/B testing common.

Rating: Root Cause Analysis (Game Bugs, WebGL Issues)
1 - Lagging: Bug root causes rarely identified. Same WebGL issues recur. No post-mortems. Firefighting mode constant. Learning minimal.
2 - Developing: Bug causes identified sometimes. WebGL issues partially understood. Occasional post-mortems. Some lessons captured.
3 - Proficient: Bug root causes systematically identified. WebGL issues diagnosed via profiling tools. Monthly bug analysis; action items tracked. Team learns from patterns.
4 - Exemplary: Root cause analysis rigorous and proactive. WebGL profiling automated (frame time, draw calls, memory). Quarterly retrospectives drive prevention. Rare bug recurrence.

Rating: Escalation & Response (Game-Breaking Issues)
1 - Lagging: No escalation process. Game-breaking bugs take days to resolve. Stakeholders blindsided. No incident response plan.
2 - Developing: Escalation path exists but unclear. Game-breaking bugs resolved in 1-2 days. Stakeholders notified late. Informal incident response.
3 - Proficient: Escalation process defined (notify stakeholders within 1 hour). Game-breaking bugs fixed within 24 hours. Stakeholders kept updated. Incident response checklist.
4 - Exemplary: Escalation automatic; stakeholders notified in minutes. Game-breaking bugs hotfixed in <4 hours. Transparent status updates. Formal incident response with blameless post-mortems.

Rating: Game Platform Health (WebGL Builds, Browser Compatibility)
1 - Lagging: WebGL builds unreliable. Browser compatibility inconsistent across Chrome/Firefox/Safari. Builds fail randomly. No monitoring or alerting.
2 - Developing: WebGL builds mostly work. Browser compatibility tested manually but incomplete. Occasional build failures. No monitoring.
3 - Proficient: WebGL builds automated and reliable. Browser compatibility tested across major browsers. Build success >99%. Manual monitoring of live games.
4 - Exemplary: WebGL builds fully automated with pre-flight checks. Compatibility tested on 10+ browser versions. Zero build failures. Automated monitoring; alerts on performance regression.

Rating: Testing & Deployment (WebGL Builds, CDN Deployment)
1 - Lagging: Minimal automated testing. Manual QA sporadic. WebGL builds deployed without validation. CDN deployment error-prone. Rollback difficult.
2 - Developing: Unit tests for core systems. Manual QA done before release. WebGL builds validated by developer. CDN deployment automated but risky. Rollback possible.
3 - Proficient: Unit + integration tests written. QA performed systematically before release. WebGL builds tested on multiple devices. CDN deployment via CI/CD. Rollback practiced.
4 - Exemplary: Test coverage >80%. Automated E2E tests for game scenarios. QA gated release; zero-defect mindset. WebGL builds and CDN deployment fully automated. Rollback instant.

Rating: Code/Asset Review & Turnaround
1 - Lagging: Code/asset reviews ad-hoc or absent. Pull request turnaround slow (3-5 days). Reviews superficial. Asset quality inconsistent.
2 - Developing: Code reviews done but turnaround slow (1-2 days). Asset reviews informal. Reviews catch some issues. Quality variable.
3 - Proficient: Code reviews completed within 24 hours. Asset reviews rigorous; LOD models, texture optimization checked. Review process clear. Quality consistent.
4 - Exemplary: Code reviews completed within 4 hours; async-friendly. Asset reviews thorough; automated checks (polygon count, texture size). Feedback detailed and mentoring-oriented.

Rating: Requirement/Game Design Document Clarity
1 - Lagging: Game Design Document vague or missing. Requirements constantly re-interpreted. Programmer/designer misalignment frequent. Rework common.
2 - Developing: GDD exists but lacks detail. Requirements somewhat clear; some re-interpretation. Occasional programmer/designer misalignment. Moderate rework.
3 - Proficient: GDD detailed with sections: mechanics, art style, UI, progression. Requirements clear; mock-ups included. Designer and programmer aligned. Minimal rework.
4 - Exemplary: GDD comprehensive with visual mockups, interactive prototypes, and behavior trees. Requirements crystal-clear. Co-authored by designer and lead programmer. Zero re-interpretation.

Rating: Build/Export Friction (Unity WebGL Build Times, Asset Optimization)
1 - Lagging: WebGL builds take 15+ minutes. Asset optimization manual and slow. Export errors block releases. Developers frustrated; productivity low.
2 - Developing: WebGL builds take 8-10 minutes. Asset optimization partially automated. Export occasionally problematic. Moderate friction.
3 - Proficient: WebGL builds take 3-5 minutes. Asset optimization automated (texture atlasing, compression). Export reliable. Minimal friction; builds don't block sprint.
4 - Exemplary: WebGL builds take <2 minutes (incremental). Asset pipeline fully optimized (automated LOD generation, draw call optimization). Export one-click. Zero friction; developers never wait.

Rating: Bottleneck Management (Art Pipeline, Programming Pipeline)
1 - Lagging: Art pipeline is constant bottleneck; programmers idle waiting for assets. Programming pipeline blocks design iteration. No visibility. Cross-discipline waits common.
2 - Developing: Bottlenecks occur; sometimes art, sometimes programming. Visibility partial. Waits cause minor delays. Communication limited.
3 - Proficient: Bottlenecks identified and tracked. Art and programming pipelines balanced. Cross-discipline collaboration prevents waits. Communication strong.
4 - Exemplary: Bottlenecks proactively prevented. Pipeline synchronized; art and code delivery in lockstep. Bottleneck forecasting in ClickUp. Zero idle time; team always productive.

Rating: Game Architecture (Unity Patterns, Clean Code, Scriptable Objects, ECS)
1 - Lagging: Code is procedural spaghetti. No use of Scriptable Objects or prefabs. Architecture unmaintainable. Entity Component System not used. Technical debt spiraling.
2 - Developing: Some Unity patterns used (prefabs, basic components). Scriptable Objects used occasionally. Architecture improving but inconsistent. ECS not yet adopted. Moderate technical debt.
3 - Proficient: Clean architecture with components and Scriptable Objects for game settings. Patterns consistent (MVC, Singleton where appropriate). ECS considered for future. Technical debt tracked and managed.
4 - Exemplary: Architecture exemplary; Scriptable Objects drive game configuration (levels, enemies, progression). ECS fully adopted where beneficial. Code is self-documenting. New features don't accrue debt.

Rating: Documentation (Game Design Docs, Technical Docs)
1 - Lagging: Documentation non-existent or severely outdated. Code undocumented; inline comments sparse. New developers struggle to onboard.
2 - Developing: Documentation exists but incomplete. Code partially documented. New developers take weeks to ramp.
3 - Proficient: Game Design Document comprehensive and current. Code documented; class/function comments clear. Architecture documented. New developers ramp in 1-2 weeks.
4 - Exemplary: GDD living and comprehensive. Code self-documenting with excellent comments. Architecture diagrams up-to-date. Asset pipeline documented. Onboarding takes 3-5 days.

Rating: Review & Mentorship (Code and Asset Reviews)
1 - Lagging: Reviews critical and demoralizing. No mentorship. Junior developers receive no guidance. Growth stalled.
2 - Developing: Reviews constructive but hurried. Mentorship ad-hoc. Junior developers get occasional guidance.
3 - Proficient: Reviews detailed, kind, and actionable. Mentorship structured (weekly 1-on-1s for juniors). Growth clear. Skill advancement tracked.
4 - Exemplary: Reviews exemplary; feedback moves conversation forward. Mentorship proactive and tailored. Junior developers thrive. Skill gaps intentionally closed.

Rating: Technical/Asset Debt (Tracking and Paydown)
1 - Lagging: Technical debt ignored or hidden. Asset debt accumulates (outdated models, unoptimized textures). Debt eventually crashes productivity.
2 - Developing: Technical debt acknowledged but not tracked formally. Asset debt visible but unprioritized. Debt paydown reactive.
3 - Proficient: Technical/asset debt tracked in ClickUp as 'debt' backlog. 15-20% sprint capacity allocated to debt paydown. Debt trends monitored.
4 - Exemplary: Debt tracked meticulously by category (code, assets, infrastructure). 20-25% capacity reserved for debt. Debt trends reverse quarterly. Paydown proactive prevents crises.

Rating: Debt Visibility (Unity Version Debt, Asset Optimization Debt, Deprecated APIs)
1 - Lagging: Debt invisible or ignored. Unity version outdated; deprecated APIs in use. Asset optimization debt unknown. Surprises frequent.
2 - Developing: Debt loosely visible. Unity version moderately outdated. Some deprecated APIs remain. Asset optimization partial. Occasional surprises.
3 - Proficient: Debt tracked in ClickUp (Unity version, API usage, asset optimization needs). Quarterly reviews of debt trends. Surprises rare.
4 - Exemplary: Debt fully visible with automated tooling. Deprecated API usage detected via linting. Asset optimization tracked (draw calls, memory). Debt trends visible in dashboards.

Rating: Strategic Allocation (Capacity for Debt Paydown)
1 - Lagging: No capacity allocated to debt. Crisis mode constant. New features take 2x longer due to debt.
2 - Developing: 10% capacity informally allocated to debt. Some paydown but inconsistent. Features occasionally slowed by debt.
3 - Proficient: 15-20% capacity formally allocated to debt per sprint. Debt paydown scheduled and tracked. Feature velocity maintained.
4 - Exemplary: Strategic debt investments planned quarterly. Capacity flexible (20-30%) based on risk. Debt investments prevent future crises.

Rating: Risk Mitigation (Unity Licensing Changes, WebGL Browser Support)
1 - Lagging: No mitigation plans. Surprised by Unity licensing changes or browser deprecations. Reactive scrambling.
2 - Developing: Some awareness of risks. Loose contingency planning. Occasional mitigation attempt.
3 - Proficient: Risks identified quarterly (licensing, browser trends). Mitigation plans drafted. Risk register maintained.
4 - Exemplary: Risk registry comprehensive and reviewed monthly. Mitigation plans detailed and tested. Contingencies for major risks (e.g., browser API deprecations). Team prepared.

Rating: Prevention (Proactive Architecture Decisions)
1 - Lagging: Prevention not discussed. Architecture decisions made locally without long-term thinking. Debt preventable but not prevented.
2 - Developing: Some prevention discussion. Architecture decisions mostly sound but gaps exist. Preventable debt sometimes accrues.
3 - Proficient: Architecture decisions reviewed for future-proofing. Design patterns chosen to prevent debt. Prevention is cultural norm.
4 - Exemplary: Prevention is first-class concern. Architecture anticipates trends (WebGPU adoption, mobile growth). Decisions systematically reviewed for 3-year impact.

Rating: Mentorship & Growth (Game Dev Skill Development)
1 - Lagging: No structured mentorship. Junior developers left to figure things out. High turnover of juniors. Growth stagnant.
2 - Developing: Mentorship ad-hoc and informal. Juniors learn slowly. Some turnover. Occasional skill advancement.
3 - Proficient: Structured mentorship: weekly 1-on-1s with junior devs. Clear skill development plans. Mentors assigned for C#, Blender, game design. Turnover low; growth visible.
4 - Exemplary: Proactive mentorship culture. Skill development plans detailed and achieved. Mentors deeply invested. Juniors quickly promoted. Turnover minimal; engagement high.

Rating: Knowledge Sharing (Critical in Small Team)
1 - Lagging: Knowledge siloed; key systems known only to one person. Bus factor high (1-2 people). Knowledge loss on departure.
2 - Developing: Knowledge mostly shared; some silos. Bus factor 2-3. Knowledge transfer informal.
3 - Proficient: Knowledge sharing systematic (wiki, code comments, demos). Bus factor >3 for all systems. Monthly tech talks. Knowledge transfer ritualized.
4 - Exemplary: Knowledge sharing exceptional. Every system documented and demystified. Bus factor >4. Bi-weekly tech talks. Institutional memory strong.

Rating: Tech Stack Evolution (Unity Updates, Blender Advances, WebGPU)
1 - Lagging: Tech stack stagnant. Unity version outdated. Blender skills not updated. WebGPU not evaluated. Falling behind industry.
2 - Developing: Tech stack updated occasionally. Unity version recent but not latest. Blender training sporadic. Industry trends loosely tracked.
3 - Proficient: Tech stack updated annually. Unity on recent version; WebGPU evaluated for roadmap. Blender training quarterly. Industry trends tracked quarterly.
4 - Exemplary: Tech stack strategic and forward-looking. Unity version latest (or pinned strategically). WebGPU adoption planned. Blender mastery advanced. Trend monitoring monthly.

Rating: Skill Diversity (Programming, Design, 3D Art Cross-Training)
1 - Lagging: Team siloed: programmers don't know design/art; artists don't understand code. Cross-training absent. Single-point failures.
2 - Developing: Some cross-training. Programmers aware of design; designers understand basic code. Asset pipeline knowledge broader.
3 - Proficient: Cross-training encouraged. Programmers can author levels in Unity; designers understand C# basics; modelers grasp optimization. Skill depth and breadth both strong.
4 - Exemplary: Cross-training systematic. Programmers prototyped mechanics in Blender; designers code game design features in C#; modelers understand shader optimization. T-shaped skills.

Rating: Psychological Safety (Safe to Experiment, Fail, Speak Up)
1 - Lagging: Team afraid to fail. Failures blamed and punished. Speaking up risky; ideas dismissed. Creativity suppressed. Defensive culture.
2 - Developing: Team mostly safe to experiment. Some blame on failure. Ideas usually heard. Moderate creativity. Generally open culture.
3 - Proficient: Team feels safe failing; failures treated as learning. Ideas heard and valued. Experimentation encouraged. Creativity high. Psychologically safe culture.
4 - Exemplary: Blameless post-mortems normalize failure. Failing fast encouraged. All ideas seriously considered. Risk-taking celebrated. Vulnerability and authenticity high.

Rating: Purpose & Impact (Games Have High Visibility)
1 - Lagging: Team sees games as 'work' with no visibility. Player impact unknown. Purpose unclear. Motivation extrinsic. Disengagement risk.
2 - Developing: Purpose somewhat clear. Games visible internally. Player impact loosely understood. Motivation mixed.
3 - Proficient: Game impact visible to team (player metrics, testimonials). Purpose clear: 'entertain X player type'. Team proud. Motivation intrinsic.
4 - Exemplary: Game impact celebrated widely (player testimonials, engagement metrics, revenue impact). Purpose inspirational. Team emotionally invested. Motivation deeply intrinsic.

Rating: Retention & Engagement (Game Dev Requires Creative Fulfillment)
1 - Lagging: Turnover high (30%+ annually). Disengagement visible. Creative fulfillment lacking. Team bored.
2 - Developing: Turnover moderate (15-20%). Engagement improving. Creative fulfillment partially met.
3 - Proficient: Turnover low (<10%). Engagement consistently high. Creative fulfillment clear; team excited. Game dev is dream job for team.
4 - Exemplary: Turnover near-zero. Engagement exceptional. Creative fulfillment maximum. Team deeply invested. Game dev is calling for team.

Rating: Recognition & Rewards (Celebrating Wins)
1 - Lagging: Wins rarely acknowledged. Team contributions invisible. No rewards or celebrations. Morale suffers.
2 - Developing: Some recognition; occasional celebration of milestones. Recognition inconsistent. Morale adequate.
3 - Proficient: Wins celebrated publicly (launch parties, demo showcases). Recognition timely and genuine. Rewards aligned with contribution. Morale high.
4 - Exemplary: Wins celebrated lavishly. Recognition is specific, public, and authentic. Rewards generous and tied to impact. Team feels valued and celebrated.

Rating: Game Knowledge Repository (Reusable Game Systems, Asset Libraries)
1 - Lagging: No knowledge repository. Game systems rebuilt from scratch each project. Asset library non-existent. Inefficiency high.
2 - Developing: Loose knowledge collection. Some code reuse but informal. Asset library minimal. Moderate inefficiency.
3 - Proficient: Knowledge repository on wiki (game systems, code libraries, design patterns). Asset library curated and organized. Reuse strong.
4 - Exemplary: Knowledge repository comprehensive and discoverable. Code libraries versioned and documented. Asset library extensive (character models, particle effects, UI templates). Reuse standard.

Rating: Cross-Division Sharing (Game Dev Insights to Other Teams)
1 - Lagging: Game dev silos isolated. No knowledge shared with other groups. Learnings not exported. Opportunity cost high.
2 - Developing: Occasional sharing. Cross-division communication limited. Some learnings exported.
3 - Proficient: Regular sharing via demos and tech talks. Game dev patterns shared with other teams. Collaborations initiated.
4 - Exemplary: Game dev is thought leader across company. Patterns actively shared. Cross-division R&D projects common. Game innovation influences product strategy.

Rating: R&D & Experimentation (Game Mechanics Prototyping, Game Jams)
1 - Lagging: R&D absent. No game jams or prototyping time. Innovation stagnant. Team creatively starved.
2 - Developing: Ad-hoc experimentation. Occasional game jams (every 6+ months). Some innovation. Limited novelty.
3 - Proficient: Dedicated R&D time (5-10% capacity). Monthly game jams on new mechanics. Innovation steady. Team energized.
4 - Exemplary: Strategic R&D program (10-15% capacity). Bi-weekly game jams. Innovation continuous. Prototypes regularly released to players. Team creatively fulfilled.

Rating: Industry Game Dev Trends (Tracking and Adoption)
1 - Lagging: Industry trends not tracked. Tech and design trends missed. Falling behind peers. No competitive awareness.
2 - Developing: Industry trends loosely followed. Some adoption of trends. Moderate competitive awareness.
3 - Proficient: Industry trends tracked quarterly (WebGPU, cross-platform tools, async multiplayer). Adoption planned. Competitive awareness good.
4 - Exemplary: Industry trends monitored continuously. Adoption proactive (early adopter where strategic). Competitive intelligence drives roadmap. Thought leadership in niche.

Rating: Vision Translation (Connecting Fun Games to Business Value)
1 - Lagging: Games are fun but business value unclear. ROI invisible. Games seen as side projects. Strategic importance low.
2 - Developing: Games moderately fun with some business alignment. ROI somewhat visible. Strategic importance emerging.
3 - Proficient: Games fun and aligned with business strategy (e.g., player acquisition, engagement, retention). ROI clear. Strategic importance established.
4 - Exemplary: Games are flagship products with clear business and creative value. ROI measured and maximized. Games drive strategy. Creative and commercial excellence.

Rating: Strategic Initiative (Roadmap and Long-term Direction)
1 - Lagging: No game roadmap or vision beyond next release. Direction unclear. Reactive development. No competitive strategy.
2 - Developing: Loose roadmap; direction somewhat clear. Mostly reactive with some planning. Basic competitive awareness.
3 - Proficient: Game roadmap clear (12-24 months). Strategic vision defined (e.g., 'best casual puzzle games on web'). Proactive development. Competitive strategy documented.
4 - Exemplary: Strategic roadmap compelling and inspiring (3-5 year vision). Direction unwavering. Proactive market shaping. Industry-leading strategy.

Rating: Impact Measurement (Player Engagement, Completion Rates, Business KPIs)
1 - Lagging: Impact unmeasured or invisible. Player metrics not collected. Business KPIs unknown. Decisions made by intuition.
2 - Developing: Impact partially measured. Player metrics collected informally. Business KPIs loosely tracked. Some data-informed decisions.
3 - Proficient: Player engagement tracked (DAU, session length). Completion rates measured. Business KPIs visible (revenue, CAC, LTV). Decisions data-informed.
4 - Exemplary: Impact measured comprehensively and in real-time. Engagement analyzed by cohort and player segment. A/B testing standard. All decisions data-driven.

Rating: Narrative Ownership (Owning the Game Narrative)
1 - Lagging: Game narrative vague or owned by stakeholders. Creative vision not owned by game team. Direction feels external. Team not invested.
2 - Developing: Game narrative roughly defined. Team owns most creative decisions. Occasional stakeholder override. Team somewhat invested.
3 - Proficient: Game narrative clear and owned by game team. Creative vision strong and defended. Stakeholders trust team direction. Team fully invested.
4 - Exemplary: Game narrative is team's creative masterpiece. Team owns all creative decisions. Stakeholder input solicited but team-led. Team passionately invested.

Rating: Issue Resolution (Game-Breaking Bugs, Performance Crises)
1 - Lagging: Game-breaking bugs take weeks to resolve. Performance crises cause downtime. No resolution process. Stakeholders left hanging.
2 - Developing: Game-breaking bugs resolved in 3-5 days. Performance crises fixed in 24-48 hours. Informal resolution process.
3 - Proficient: Game-breaking bugs resolved within 24 hours. Performance crises fixed within 4-8 hours. Escalation and resolution process clear.
4 - Exemplary: Game-breaking bugs hotfixed in <4 hours. Performance crises resolved within 2 hours. Resolution process is second nature.

Rating: Proactive Risk Mitigation (WebGL Compatibility, Unity Updates)
1 - Lagging: Risks not identified until they occur. WebGL browser changes cause surprises. Unity updates break the game. Reactive crisis mode.
2 - Developing: Risks loosely identified. Some mitigation planning. WebGL compatibility issues occasionally prevented. Unity upgrades planned with some buffer.
3 - Proficient: Risks identified quarterly. Mitigation plans developed. WebGL compatibility tested proactively. Unity upgrade path planned. Crisis prevention strong.
4 - Exemplary: Risk mitigation proactive and continuous. WebGL updates tracked and tested instantly. Unity roadmap monitored; upgrade path strategic. Crises rare.

Rating: Trade-off Transparency (Feature vs Polish, Scope vs Schedule)
1 - Lagging: Trade-offs made but not transparent. Team blindsided by decisions. Stakeholders frustrated by sudden pivots.
2 - Developing: Trade-offs sometimes transparent. Communication okay but ad-hoc. Some stakeholder frustration.
3 - Proficient: Trade-offs clearly articulated: 'Feature X deferred to focus on performance'. All stakeholders understand rationale. Decisions feel fair.
4 - Exemplary: Trade-off decisions exemplary. Transparent frameworks (MoSCoW prioritization). Rationale deeply communicated. Stakeholders become advocates.

Rating: Crisis Ownership (Taking Responsibility, Not Blaming)
1 - Lagging: Crises blamed on luck, stakeholders, or tools. Team defensive. Post-mortems absent or blame-focused. Learning minimal.
2 - Developing: Crises mostly owned but with some external blame. Post-mortems rare. Some lessons captured.
3 - Proficient: Crises fully owned by game team. Blameless post-mortems standard. Clear action items from crises. Learning clear.
4 - Exemplary: Crises treated as learning opportunities. Blameless culture deeply embedded. Post-mortems rapid and insightful. Systems improve from each crisis.

Rating: Stakeholder Communication (Milestone Demos, Progress)
1 - Lagging: Communication sparse. Stakeholders surprised by game state. Progress invisible. Trust eroding. Stakeholders frustrated.
2 - Developing: Communication ad-hoc. Demos quarterly or sporadic. Progress somewhat visible. Trust variable.
3 - Proficient: Bi-weekly milestone demos with playable builds. Weekly status updates in ClickUp. Stakeholders always informed. Trust strong.
4 - Exemplary: Weekly playable demos. Daily progress visibility. Proactive stakeholder communication. Stakeholders feel invested and informed.

Rating: Response Timeliness (Answering Stakeholder Questions)
1 - Lagging: Questions answered in days (if at all). Stakeholders frustrated. Communication feels unresponsive.
2 - Developing: Questions answered in 24-48 hours. Responsiveness adequate but slow.
3 - Proficient: Questions answered within 24 hours. Responsiveness strong. Stakeholders feel heard.
4 - Exemplary: Questions answered within 4 hours (or immediately for urgent). Responsiveness exceptional. Stakeholders feel valued.

Rating: Internal Knowledge Sharing (Design Docs, Tech Talks, Runbooks)
1 - Lagging: Knowledge not shared; team works in silos. Onboarding difficult. Institutional knowledge at risk.
2 - Developing: Knowledge shared informally; some documentation. Onboarding slow. Institutional knowledge at moderate risk.
3 - Proficient: Knowledge shared systematically via wiki, tech talks, code comments. Onboarding clear. Institutional knowledge preserved.
4 - Exemplary: Knowledge sharing culture exceptional. Weekly tech talks, comprehensive runbooks. Onboarding takes 3-5 days. Knowledge democratized.

Rating: Feedback Loop Quality (Playtesting, Player Feedback, Iteration)
1 - Lagging: Playtesting minimal. Player feedback not collected or ignored. No iteration loop. Game stagnates.
2 - Developing: Playtesting monthly. Player feedback collected but prioritization weak. Slow iteration.
3 - Proficient: Playtesting bi-weekly. Player feedback systematically captured and prioritized. Rapid iteration (weekly updates). Game improves visibly.
4 - Exemplary: Playtesting weekly with diverse player cohorts. Feedback drives decisions. Iteration continuous (daily updates possible). Game evolves based on players.

Rating: Skill Gap Closure (Game Dev is Multidisciplinary)
1 - Lagging: Skill gaps not identified or addressed. Team weak in core areas (C#, Blender, game design). Growth stalled.
2 - Developing: Skill gaps loosely identified. Some training occurs. Growth moderate. Gaps persist.
3 - Proficient: Skill gaps tracked and addressed. Training budget allocated. Gaps systematically closed (courses, mentorship, projects). Team constantly improving.
4 - Exemplary: Skill gaps identified and closed proactively. Training is cultural norm. Learning budget generous. T-shaped skill development standard.

Rating: Process Improvement (Iterating on Game Dev Processes)
1 - Lagging: Processes not discussed. Chaos the default. Retrospectives absent. Improvement stagnant. Friction high.
2 - Developing: Processes exist but not optimized. Retrospectives quarterly or ad-hoc. Modest improvement. Some friction remains.
3 - Proficient: Processes reviewed bi-weekly in retrospectives. Action items tracked. Process improvements implemented regularly. Friction declining. Team velocity improving.
4 - Exemplary: Processes continuously refined. Weekly retrospectives. Improvement is cultural obsession. Friction minimized. Team velocity accelerating.

Rating: Automation & Tooling (Unity Editor Tools, Asset Pipelines, CI/CD for Games)
1 - Lagging: Automation absent. Manual processes everywhere (builds, asset optimization, testing). Inefficiency high. Developer frustration high.
2 - Developing: Some automation (builds). Asset pipelines partially automated. Manual testing remains. Moderate inefficiency.
3 - Proficient: Build pipeline fully automated. Asset pipelines automated (texture compression, LOD generation). Unit tests automated. CI/CD for WebGL deployments. Good efficiency.
4 - Exemplary: Comprehensive automation: builds, assets, tests, deployments. Unity Editor tools custom-built (e.g., batch importer). Developers never do manual work. Productivity exceptional.

Rating: Learning Culture (Books, Courses, Conferences, Experimentation)
1 - Lagging: Learning not valued. Training budget minimal or absent. Team stagnates. Innovation low. Talent leaves.
2 - Developing: Learning somewhat valued. Training budget modest. Occasional courses. Learning culture emerging.
3 - Proficient: Learning actively supported. Training budget allocated per dev ($2-5K annually). Courses encouraged. Game dev conferences attended. Learning culture strong.
4 - Exemplary: Learning is cultural obsession. Generous training budget ($10K+ per dev). Conference attendance supported. Book clubs, workshops, experiments encouraged. Learning celebrated.

Rating: Change Adoption Rate (New Tools, Processes, Tech)
1 - Lagging: Changes resisted. Team clings to status quo. Adoption slow. Innovation blocked. Inertia high.
2 - Developing: Changes sometimes adopted. Initial resistance but eventual buy-in. Adoption slow but steady. Some inertia.
3 - Proficient: Changes adopted within 2-4 weeks. Team understands rationale. Adoption smooth. Minimal inertia.
4 - Exemplary: Changes adopted within days. Team champions new approaches. Adoption rapid and enthusiastic. Inertia eliminated.

Rating: Resistance Management (Addressing Change Concerns)
1 - Lagging: Concerns dismissed or ignored. Team resentful. Change imposed top-down. Resistance festers.
2 - Developing: Concerns acknowledged but not always addressed. Communication present but limited. Resistance manageable.
3 - Proficient: Concerns heard and addressed transparently. Rationale well-communicated. Team involved in decisions. Resistance minimal.
4 - Exemplary: Concerns deeply understood and respected. Change co-created with team. Team advocates for changes. Resistance nonexistent.

Rating: Vision Casting (Communicating Long-term Direction)
1 - Lagging: Vision absent or unclear. Team confused about direction. No inspiration. Motivation low.
2 - Developing: Vision exists but not compelling. Direction somewhat clear. Inspiration moderate.
3 - Proficient: Vision clear and inspiring (e.g., 'best casual game studio on web'). Direction understood by team. Inspiration strong.
4 - Exemplary: Vision is North Star that inspires daily. Direction crystal-clear and compelling. Team emotionally connected. Inspiration pervasive.

Rating: Stability vs. Agility (Balancing Consistency with Flexibility)
1 - Lagging: Chaotic; no consistency. Or overly rigid; no flexibility. Balance missing. Team frustrated.
2 - Developing: Some balance but inconsistent. Processes too rigid or too loose.
3 - Proficient: Good balance: core processes stable, game design flexible. Team can iterate rapidly without breaking foundations. Agility within structure.
4 - Exemplary: Exceptional balance: core processes rock-solid, game mechanics incredibly agile. Rapid iteration without loss of stability. Stability and agility coexist.

Rating: Team Morale & Energy (Game Dev Passion)
1 - Lagging: Morale low. Team disengaged. Passion absent. High turnover risk. Work feels like work.
2 - Developing: Morale adequate. Team functional but not enthusiastic. Passion fluctuates. Turnover moderate.
3 - Proficient: Morale high. Team energized by games. Passion visible. Turnover low. Work feels like play.
4 - Exemplary: Morale exceptional. Team radiates passion for games. Energy contagious. Turnover near-zero. Team loves their work.

Rating: Mentorship & Empowerment (Developing Team Potential)
1 - Lagging: Mentorship absent. Team micromanaged or left to flounder. Empowerment lacking. Potential untapped.
2 - Developing: Mentorship ad-hoc. Team somewhat empowered. Potential partially developed.
3 - Proficient: Mentorship structured and impactful. Team highly empowered. Potential systematically developed. Growth visible.
4 - Exemplary: Mentorship exemplary. Team deeply empowered to make decisions. Potential fully developed and unleashed. Remarkable growth.

Rating: Vulnerability & Trust (Safe to Share Concerns, Ask for Help)
1 - Lagging: Team defensive. Asking for help seen as weakness. Vulnerability punished. Isolation high. Trust low.
2 - Developing: Team mostly safe. Some vulnerability. Trust present but fragile. Help-seeking somewhat normalized.
3 - Proficient: Team vulnerably honest. Asking for help normal and encouraged. Trust strong. Psychological safety high.
4 - Exemplary: Extreme vulnerability and trust. Team shares failures, doubts, ideas openly. Help-seeking celebrated. Trust unbreakable.

Rating: Recognition of Potential (Seeing and Developing Talent)
1 - Lagging: Talent overlooked. Potential not recognized. No career path. Top talent leaves.
2 - Developing: Some talent recognized. Career paths loosely defined. Top talent retention moderate.
3 - Proficient: Talent consistently recognized and developed. Clear career paths (senior programmer, tech lead, design lead). Potential cultivation intentional.
4 - Exemplary: Talent discovery is core competency. Hidden potential proactively surfaced. Career paths clear and exciting. Top talent retains and thrives.

Rating: Innovative Game Solutions (Creative Game Mechanics, Technical Workarounds)
1 - Lagging: Game mechanics generic or copied. No unique solutions. Innovation absent. Games forgettable. Competitive disadvantage.
2 - Developing: Game mechanics somewhat creative. Some unique workarounds. Limited innovation. Games have some novelty.
3 - Proficient: Game mechanics fresh and engaging. Creative technical workarounds (WebGL optimization tricks, Blender pipeline innovations). Innovation clear. Games memorable.
4 - Exemplary: Game mechanics groundbreaking and addictive. Technical innovations become case studies (e.g., elegant ECS patterns, WebGL streaming architecture). Games industry-leading and distinctive.

Rating: Blocker Resolution (Asset Pipeline Blocks, Platform Constraints)
1 - Lagging: Blockers stall development for days/weeks. Asset pipeline constrained. Platform limitations not addressed. Frustration high.
2 - Developing: Blockers typically resolved in 1-2 days. Asset pipeline mostly flows. Platform limitations occasionally worked around.
3 - Proficient: Blockers resolved within 4-8 hours. Asset pipeline flowing smoothly. Platform constraints systematically addressed. Forward momentum maintained.
4 - Exemplary: Blockers eliminated proactively; resolution <2 hours if they occur. Asset pipeline optimized end-to-end. Platform constraints turned into advantages. Zero stalls.

Rating: Culture of Experimentation (Game Jams, Prototyping, Trying New Approaches)
1 - Lagging: Experimentation discouraged. 'No time to experiment'. Status quo enforced. Innovation stagnant. Team creatively starved.
2 - Developing: Experimentation loosely encouraged. Occasional game jams. Some new approaches tried. Moderate creativity.
3 - Proficient: Experimentation encouraged and time-allocated (5-10% capacity). Monthly game jams on new mechanics. New approaches tried regularly. Team creatively energized.
4 - Exemplary: Experimentation is cultural expectation (10-15% capacity). Bi-weekly game jams. Prototyping rapid and celebrated. Risk-taking rewarded. Team creatively fulfilled.

Rating: Resourceful Orchestration (Making Do With Limited Resources, Creative Problem-Solving)
1 - Lagging: Resource constraints paralyze team. Excuses made. Problems unsolved. Frustration high. Morale low.
2 - Developing: Resource constraints manageable. Some creative problem-solving. Adequate solutions found. Morale adequate.
3 - Proficient: Resource constraints anticipated and addressed creatively. Team problem-solves effectively (e.g., free/open-source assets, efficient pipelines). Constraints minimize impact. Morale good.
4 - Exemplary: Resource constraints are obstacles to overcome creatively. Team resourcefully solves problems without additional budget (asset sharing, optimization, process efficiency). Morale exceptional. Constraints become advantages.
